case_name: Phoebe Charmaine Pickering   v Cambridge University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust [2022] EWHC 1171 (QB) (17 May 2022)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The Claimant is a patient who attended Addenbrookes Hospital in Cambridge for treatment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Defendant runs Addenbrookes Hospital and is responsible for the standards of clinical care provided there.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Defendant had admitted that it had been negligent in failing to treat the Claimant with immediate low molecular weight Heparin by injection in A&E at or before 01.44 am on 25 September 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Claimant had suffered a massive stroke. A blood clot or clots had travelled from her left atrial appendage to her brain and lodged in the left middle cerebral artery and the left internal carotid artery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Michael is an eminent consultant neurologist who qualified in medicine at Oxford, is a fellow of the Royal College of Physicians and worked for 30 years as an NHS consultant in the South East Thames area and then at Kings College Hospital in South London.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: He advised that both the leg clot and the stroke clot " were attributable to the same mechanism, namely embolic fragments being thrown off from a common cardiac source. "
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Michael

evidence_index.text: Dr. Giallombardo approached the Claimant's case on the basis that she would have been prescribed Apixaban on 25 September 2015, which is not the pleaded case.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Giallombardo

evidence_index.text: Dr. Michael and Dr. Giallombardo agreed that the Claimant was at significant risk of further emboli after she had suffered the first blood clot in her leg.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Michael and Dr. Giallombardo

evidence_index.text: The Defendant's hospital breached its duty of care to the Claimant, as it admits, in failing to start the Claimant on injected Heparin at just before 2:00 AM on the morning of 25 September 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: I find as a fact that had the Defendant administered Heparin and then therapeutic Warfarin or modern anti-coagulation it would probably have taken between three and four weeks (roughly 25 days) to dissolve the Claimant's left atrial appendage clot.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: I enter judgment for the Claimant.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Intermittent episodes of right leg turning cold and white
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient experienced intermittent episodes of her right leg turning cold and white, with paraesthesia and pain, lasting about 5 minutes each and resolving completely.

focused_exam.legs: Near normal appearance of both legs; pedal pulses bilaterally present; feet slightly cold equally; normal power and sensation in knees and ankles; no evidence of abnormal discolouration.

initial_differential: Resolved ischaemic event of right leg
critical_findings: Intermittent episodes of leg turning cold and white

requestables.name: Neurology Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with neurologist for stroke risk assessment

reveal.decision_impact: Missed opportunity to assess stroke risk

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to A&E with leg symptoms

timeline_phases.name: A&E Workup
timeline_phases.description: Further assessment and decision-making in A&E

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in A&E with symptoms suggestive of embolic event
decision_points.prompt: What immediate treatment should be administered?

options.clinical_reasoning: Heparin would reduce the risk of further embolic events

options.description: Discharge with advice to take Aspirin
options.clinical_reasoning: Aspirin alone is insufficient for high embolic risk

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant discharged patient with advice to take Aspirin

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established Heparin as necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to administer Heparin increased risk of stroke
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Heparin administration would have reduced embolic risk

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to administer Heparin
Causation established - earlier treatment would have prevented stroke

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Heparin would have stabilized the clot and prevented stroke

factual_timeline.event: Ambulance call for right leg symptoms
Patient admitted to hospital with leg ischemia
